coredata.dc:title: Heterogeneous Fenton-like degradation of ofloxacin over a wide pH range of 3.6–10.0 over modified mesoporous iron oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xike
Jin, Hang
Nie, Yulun
Zhou, Zhaoxin
Yang, Chao
Li, Yong
Wang, Yanxin
coredata.dc:description: To develop an efficient heterogeneous Fenton catalyst over a wide pH range without any external energy input is still a challenging work. In this study, the Fenton activity of mesoporous iron oxide was firstly extended to pH of 3.6–10.0 by the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Cu modification (CQDs/Cu-MIO). The characterization studies indicated that CQDs/Cu-MIO had a typical mesoporous structure and CQDs was highly dispersed on the surface of catalyst. While Cu element with Cu (I) and Cu (II) was introduced in the framework of iron oxide by chemical binding of FeOCu. Without the aids of UV, CQDs/Cu-MIO exhibited an excellent efficiency and the ofloxacin (OFX) degradation followed the pseudo-first order kinetic model with a reaction constant of 0.1109min−1. Moreover, the catalyst can be reused for 6 times with good stability, in which the maximum concentration of leaching Fe and Cu ions were 0.085 and 0.015mmol/L. The optimum reaction condition was evaluated and the degradation process of OFX was further investigated by FT-IR spectrum. The reactive oxygen species involved in OFX degradation, the effects of mesoporous structure, Cu/Fe multivalent state and the excellent capability of electron transformation/energy exchange of CQDs on the enhancement of CQDs/Cu-MIO Fenton activity were also discussed. Finally, the heterogeneous Fenton reaction mechanism was proposed based on the experimental results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fenton
Mesoporous iron oxide
Cu doping
CQDs
Activity enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: OFX degradation performance in the presence of CQDs/Cu-MIO

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of different as-prepared samples: (A) Low angle and (B) high angle.
TEM images of (A) SBA-15, (B) Cu-MIO and (C) CQDs/Cu-MIO.
The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of (a) Cu-MIO and (b) CQDs/Cu-MIO.

(A) Catalytic degradation of OFX and (B) the pseudo-first-order kinetic analysis under different conditions: (a) only H2O2, (b) only CQDs/Cu-MIO, (c) M−Fe+H2O2, (d) Cu-MIO+H2O2, (e) CQDs/Cu-MIO+H2O2. (Experimental conditions: 100ml 12mg/L OFX, pH 6.4, catalyst amount 0.25g/L and H2O2 dosage 5mL/L.)
Effects of Cu content on the XRD patterns and Fenton activity of Cu-MIO towards OFX degradation.
Effects of CQDs loading on the Fenton activity of CQDs/Cu-MIO towards OFX degradation.
Effect of (A) CQDs/Cu-MIO amount and (B) H2O2 dosage on the OFX removal efficiency.
Effect of initial solution pH on the OFX degradation efficiency in CQDs/Cu-MIO-H2O2 system. (Experimental conditions: 100ml 12mg/L OFX, catalyst amount 0.25g/L and H2O2 dosage 5mL/L.)
Stability of CQDs/Cu-MIO in the multicycle degradation of OFX in the presence of H2O2. (Experimental conditions: 100ml 12mg/L OFX, pH 6.4, catalyst amount 0.25g/L and H2O2 dosage 5mL/L.)

(A) Effects of various radical scavengers on the degradation of OFX over CQDs/Cu-MIO and (B) ESR spectra of
OH and
O2 − in water or methanol system.
XPS spectrum of (a) Cu-MIO and (b) CQDs/Cu-MIO: (A) Fe, (B) Cu, (C) O and (D) C.
The proposed heterogeneous Fenton-like reaction mechanism of CQDs/Cu-MIO.

head.ce:title: Heterogeneous Fenton-like degradation of ofloxacin over a wide pH range of 3.6–10.0 over modified mesoporous iron oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Studies, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To develop an efficient heterogeneous Fenton catalyst over a wide pH range without any external energy input is still a challenging work. In this study, the Fenton activity of mesoporous iron oxide was firstly extended to pH of 3.6–10.0 by the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Cu modification (CQDs/Cu-MIO). The characterization studies indicated that CQDs/Cu-MIO had a typical mesoporous structure and CQDs was highly dispersed on the surface of catalyst. While Cu element with Cu (I) and Cu (II) was introduced in the framework of iron oxide by chemical binding of FeOCu. Without the aids of UV, CQDs/Cu-MIO exhibited an excellent efficiency and the ofloxacin (OFX) degradation followed the pseudo-first order kinetic model with a reaction constant of 0.1109min−1. Moreover, the catalyst can be reused for 6 times with good stability, in which the maximum concentration of leaching Fe and Cu ions were 0.085 and 0.015mmol/L. The optimum reaction condition was evaluated and the degradation process of OFX was further investigated by FT-IR spectrum. The reactive oxygen species involved in OFX degradation, the effects of mesoporous structure, Cu/Fe multivalent state and the excellent capability of electron transformation/energy exchange of CQDs on the enhancement of CQDs/Cu-MIO Fenton activity were also discussed. Finally, the heterogeneous Fenton reaction mechanism was proposed based on the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the frequent use of antibiotics results in serious environment issues [1]. Ofloxacin (OFX), one of the 2nd-generation of fluoroquinolone drugs, along with human’s life for its activity in against bacteria [2,3]. Moreover, OFX is also toxic to mammalian cells for it can induce oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation and DNA oxidative damage to chondrocytes [4]. In addition, it is characterized by its very poor biodegradability and thus, they commonly reach the environment due the ineffectiveness of the urban wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) for their removal. Its average concentration in WWTPs discharge and surface water in China are 780±132ng/L and 74±15ng/L, respectively [5,6]. Of course, high concentration of OFX will pose a potential threat to the underground water due to the effect of infiltration or runoff, which has caused increasing diseases such as cancer and becomes one of the main organic pollutants worldwide [7].

Advanced oxidation process as a green technology will eliminate organic pollution in underground water efficiently and environmentally friendly [8]. Fenton process, a kind of typical advanced oxidation reaction, should also be competent in the treatment of environmental contaminant [9,10]. However, there are three main disadvantages of the Fenton process. Firstly, extra energy such as UV is necessarily introduced into the reaction system to extend the working pH range. Secondly, the consumption of the reagent (Fe2+ and H2O2) is huge in conventional homogeneous Fenton and production of Fe containing sludge after the reaction. Thirdly, the conventional Fenton process requires strong acidic conditions (pH≈3), which makes the Fenton reaction efficiency decrease since the acidity of aquatic environment is usually at near neutral pH [11,12]. Some solutions were then put forward to solve these problems. To save extra energy, visible light and natural light were used to replace UV irradiation in the latest researches [13,14]. To avoid the waste of reagent and enhance the efficiency of catalyst, Fe2O3 nanoparticles mixed with Mg were synthesized and exhibited enhanced catalytic activity for the degradation of methylene blue [15]. Seyed Ghorban Hosseini et al. also fabricated mesoporous Fe2O3 nanocomposite by SBA-15 silica as template to increase active sites of the catalyst [16]. Moreover, many researches are devoted to extend the applied pH range, such as doped S [11,17], combined with amino acid [18,19] or bonded with phenols [12]. Nevertheless, most of the researches can only extend the working pH to neutral but not alkaline conditions. Therefore, it is still a challenging work to solve all the three drawbacks of classic Fenton process.

At present, the heterogeneous Fenton process provides an effective alternative to the classical homogeneous Fenton reaction. Generally, the principal factor determining catalytic efficiency in heterogeneous catalysis is the sufficiently large surface area, which can be achieved by the use of either nanoparticles or granular particles with porous character. Iron oxides (γ-Fe2O3, α-FeOOH, Fe0/Fe3O4) have been widely used in activation of H2O2 towards the removal of organic pollutants in water. Unfortunately, the process is relatively slow and inefficient at circumneutral pH values due to the limited surface reaction sites [20,21]. The porous materials with unique nanostructure had a large surface area by providing high dispersion of active component and enhancing the mass transportation [22–24]. But up to now, few studies were conducted to synthesize porous iron oxide assembled quantum dots and use it as heterogeneous Fenton catalyst. Moreover, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new class of nanomaterial that possess excellent physicochemical characteristics, fluorescence emissions, and optical properties [25,26]. It can be used as photocatalyst in organic pollutant degradation owe to the capability of electron transformation and energy exchange with supporting species, for instance, TiO2/CQDs composite stimulated by visible-light catalysis and the hydroxyapatite supported N-doped CQDs towards methylene blue degradation were developed and exhibited excellent performance [26]. TiO2/CQD nanocomposites are able to completely degrade methylene blue (50mg/mL) within 25min under visible light irradiation, where only 5% of MB is degraded when pure TiO2 is used as the photocatalyst. The modification with CQDs can obtain the rapidly electron transformation in the reaction system and improve the catalytic activity even in alkaline conditions [27]. Hence, we draw CQDs into our research to devise a kind of catalyst which can be used to catalyze Fenton process in a wide pH range without the aid of UV/ultrasonic.

In this study, the Fenton like activity of mesoporous iron oxide was enhanced by tailoring the surface electron transfer process via CQDs and Cu modification. Without UV or ultrasonic irradiation, the as-prepared CQDs/Cu-MIO exhibited an excellent performance in the ofloxacin degradation at a pH range of 3.6–10.0. The reaction mechanism and the effects of mesoporous structure, Cu and CQDs modification on the enhancement of Fenton catalytic activity was further discussed.

Mesoporous Cu doped iron oxide (Cu-MIO) was synthesized by a hard template method, in which the template of SBA-15 was prepared according to the procedure described by Chen et al. [28]. In a typical experiment, 0.4g SBA-15 was added in 5 mL deionized water and stirred for several minutes. Different amounts of FeSO4·7H2O and CuSO4·5H2O ([Cu]/[Cu+Fe]=5–25% in molar ratio) were then added into the above suspension and kept stirring for 4h. After filtered and drying overnight, the obtained powder was calcined at 600°C for 6h. Finally, SBA-15 was removed by 20mL 1M NaOH solution under boiled condition and Cu-MIO was then obtained by washing with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method [29] and were used to modify Cu-MIO as followed: a given amount of Cu-MIO was added into a CQDs containing solution, which was followed by ultra-sonication for one hour. The brown powder of CQDs/Cu-MIO was finally obtained after drying at 80°C under stirring.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Rigaku D/MAXRC X-ray diffractrometer using Cu Kα radiation (γ=0.154nm) as the X-ray source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected on a transmission electron microscope with field emission gun at 200kV (JEOL 2000EX, JEOL, Japan). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured at a Micromeritics TriStar II sorptometer, the specific surface area and pore size distribution curves were obtained using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method from the adsorption data. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a MULT1LAB2000 photoelectron spectroscopy. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed to assess different functional groups of the OFX degradation procedure using KBr pressed disks with a FTIR 650 spectrometer transmission analyzer. ICP-MS analysis was carried out on a ICAPQ01890.

The Fenton catalytic activity of as-prepared catalyst was evaluated at ambient conditions, which meant that there was no requirement of the extra energy input such UV or ultrasonic irradiation. In a typical experiment, 25mg catalyst were dispersed in 100mL 12mg/L of OFX solution, which was magnetically stirred for about 50min to establish the adsorption/desorption equilibrium [30]. Then, the desired amount of 30wt% H2O2 (the molar concentration of 30wt% H2O2 was 9.7mol/L) was added to the above suspension under continuous magnetic stirring. At given time intervals, 2mL sample was taken and filtered immediately to remove the catalyst for analysis. Then 0.1mL 0.04mol/L Na2S2O4 was added to quench destroy the residual H2O2 [31]. The concentration of OFX in filtrate was determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with an UV-DAD detector, the chromatographic separation was performed by a reverse-phase C18 column (250mm×4.6mm, 5μm). The mobile phase was composed of 15% acetonitrile and 85% ultrapure water which is acidified by 1% phosphoric acid with a flow rate of 0.5mL/min. The injection volume was 20μL. Temperature of the column chamber was maintained at 25°C and the detection wavelength was 288nm. The initial solution pH was adjusted by adding 0.1M H2SO4 or 0.1M NaOH. All the experiments were repeated three times. Methanol, N2 and NaN3 were used as radical scavengers for
OH,
O2 − and 1O2, respectively. In addition, electron spin resonance (ESR) was also used to detect the involved radicals by a FA200 ESR spectrometer. 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a nitrone spin trap which mainly combined with
OH in water system and with
O2 − in methanol system [32].

The mesoporous structure of as-prepared samples was confirmed by low-angle powder XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 1 A. Similar to SBA-15, both Cu-MIO and CQDs/Cu-MIO had a strong diffraction peak at 2 theta<1 degree, indicating the well ordered hexagonal mesoporous structure [28]. According to Fig. 1B, the XRD patterns of MIO were readily indexed to the hexagonal hematite (α-Fe2O3, JCPDS No. 33-0664). After Cu doping and modification by CQDs, Cu-MIO and CQDs/Cu-MIO still exhibited almost the same wide-angle XRD patterns with MIO and no other diffraction peak for the crystalline phases of copper oxides was observed. It indicated that Fe in MIO was successfully substituted by Cu and CQDs was highly dispersed on the surface of Cu-MIO since the peak of CQDs was not obvious in XRD patterns [33]. Fig. 2 displayed the TEM images of SBA-15, Cu-MIO and CQDs/Cu-MIO. Compared with SBA-15, the mesoporous layer with ordered channel was remained in Cu-MIO and CQDs was also obviously dispersed on Cu-MIO. The porous properties of the samples were further obtained from the Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 3 ). The type-IV isotherm according to IUPAC was found with a large hysteresis loop between the adsorption and desorption isotherms over a P/P0 range of 0.43–0.89, which further indicates the mesoporous structure of Cu-MIO and CQDs/Cu-MIO. Besides, the similar BET specific surface area and pore size distribution of Cu-MIO and CQDs/Cu-MIO (Fig. S1 and Table. S1) further confirmed that the CQDs modification should occurred outside the mesopores of MIO [22–24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: OFX degradation performance in the presence of CQDs/Cu-MIO
ce:section.ce:para: The heterogeneous Fenton-like activity of as-prepared catalysts was evaluated by the catalytic degradation of OFX at pH 6.4 with H2O2 at ambient conditions without extra energy input as shown in Fig. 4 . Obviously, only 10% of the OFX was adsorbed over CQDs/Cu-MIO and the adsorption/desorption equilibrium could be obtained at 50min (curve b). No significant degradation of OFX was observed only in the presence of H2O2 (curve a). It was interesting that OFX was more efficiently degraded due to the activation of H2O2 by the as-prepared catalysts. About 64% of OFX was removed at 100min in the MIO suspensions (curve c). In comparison, the removal efficiency was greatly enhanced and OFX could be completely degraded by Cu-MIO, especially CQDs/Cu-MIO under the same conditions (curves d and e). Moreover, the OFX abatement followed pseudo first-order kinetics and reaction rate constant k was 0.0076, 0.0405, and 0.1109min−1 for MIO, Cu-MIO and CQDs/Cu-MIO, respectively. The results indicated that the mesoporous iron oxide (MIO) can be used as Fenton catalyst and exhibited some activity towards OFX degradation. The doping of Cu element, especially the supporting of CQDs could enhance the OFX removal efficiency and removal rate significantly at neutral pH. It has been reported that the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ is the rate limiting step in the whole Fenton reaction (0.001–0.02M−1 s−1). The spontaneous electron flow from Cu+ to Fe3+ favors the Fe3+ conversion to Fe2+ and the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ by H2O2 was also easier than Fe3+ to Fe2+. Moreover, the CQDs modification can also enhance the electron transfer from Cu+ to Fe3+ and improve the Fenton catalytic activity since the CQDs have the excellent capability of electron transformation and energy exchange. Hence, CQDs/Cu-MIO was proven to be a promising heterogeneous Fenton catalyst in the remediation of OFX containing wastewater. The OFX degradation procedure was detected with FT-IR and the spectra was shown in Fig. S5. The peak characteristic of methyl/methylene (2750–3100cm−1) and naphthalene ring (1400–1600cm−1) of OFX decreased with reaction time, which indicated the breakdown of the primary aromatic structure. During the reaction, the peak at 738cm−1 which represents NH bond banding vibration appear repeatedly, shows the piperazinyl substituent was destroyed completely [40]. Meanwhile, a new peak attribute to CO groups of carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and/or ketones, and a new wide peak for the OH of carboxylic acids appeared at 1685 and 3183cm−1, respectively. Associated with another new strong peak at 1392cm−1 of carboxyl, it can be indicated that the benzene ring has been destroyed and some new carboxylic acids were formed during the degradation of OFX. At the same time, the peak at 2364cm−1 appeared after 60min represents the forming of CC during the reaction [41]. And from the decrease of TOC (Fig. S6) we can found that the TOC removal ratio achieved 78% after the 120min reaction, indicated a highly efficiency on toxicity loss.

The effects of Cu substitution and CQDs loading amount on the catalytic activity of CQDs/Cu-MIO were further investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 5 , the catalytic activity of Cu-MIO increased with the Cu content and 15% of Cu substitution showed the highest OFX removal efficiency (96% at 80min). When the Cu amount was increased to 25%, the catalytic activity of Cu-MIO started to decrease (78% at 80min). Based on the XRD analysis (inset of Fig. 5), the characteristic peak of CuO at 38.8 degree appeared when the Cu doping was 25%, which indicated that the extraframework copper species were produced in the form of aggregated copper oxide clusters. The above results confirmed that Cu-MIO with Cu doped in the framework of MIO had the highest Fenton like activity towards OFX degradation [34]. Fig. 6 depicted that the amount of CQDs also had a great influence on the Fenton activity of Cu-MIO. The removal efficiency of OFX increased to a maximum value and then decreased with the CQDs loading (0.3, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0mg/g) with a pseudo first-order reaction kinetics constant of 0.0601, 0.0885, 0.0971 and 0.0502min−1, separately. Much more amount of CQDs may aggregate to clusters and then inhibit the electron transfer process. Consequently, CQDs/Cu-MIO with 15% Cu substitution and 1.5mg/g CQDs loading was used in the following experiment. As shown in Fig. 7 , the effect of catalyst loading and H2O2 dosage on the OFX removal efficiency was also explored. Obviously, the degradation efficiency increased with the amount of CQDs/Cu-MIO (0.10–0.25g/L) due to the increase of active sites while the H2O2 dosage had almost no significant impact on the OFX degradation (0.2–10.0ml/L). To ensure the complete OFX degradation and avoid the reagent waste, 0.25g/L of CQDs/Cu-MIO and 1.0ml/L of H2O2 was chosen as the optimum reaction condition.

It is well known that pH value is one of the most significant factors in the Fenton reaction system, Fenton process is relatively slow and inefficient at circumneutral pH values because only a small fraction of the H2O2 is converted into oxidants that are capable of transforming recalcitrant contaminants [35]. Hence, to extend the high Fenton activity to a wide pH range is of crucial importance in the development of heterogeneous Fenton catalyst. As shown in Fig. 8 , CQDs/Cu-MIO always exhibited a high catalytic activity over a wide pH range of 3.6–10.0 and OFX was degraded by 91–100% at acid, neutral and even alkaline conditions after reaction for 40min. The excellent performance of CQDs/Cu-MIO at neutral and alkaline pH should be attributed to the broader working pH range of Cu doping in MIO and the rapid electron transformation property of CQDs resulting in the enhancement of H2O2 decomposition into
OH [36–39]. Fig. 9 depicted the recycling experiment of CQDs/Cu-MIO in catalytic degradation of OFX. Its performance was not remarkably decreased during the six recycle process. The leaching of Fe and Cu ions in every cycle were detected and as shown in the picture, the amount of leaching ions drop off by every cycle and become stable after the third cycle. The maximum concentration of leaching Fe and Cu ions were determined by ICP-MS and shown in Fig. S2 are about 0.085 and 0.015mmol/L, respectively, which were below their discharge limit in water (8.0 and 1.0mg/L, GB57459, 2006). Hence, the possibility of ion leaching causing the secondary pollution to treated water was not significant. XRD patterns also showed almost no decreases in the peak intensity (Fig. S3). The above results proved that CQDs/Cu-MIO exhibited an excellent catalytic activity and long-term stability in efficient removal of OFX in water. We also examined the suitability of CQDs/Cu-MIO in the real water treatment, in which tap water, water from a local lake and a pond in school were used (Fig. S4). CQDs/Cu-MIO shows a slight decrease in activity but still keeps a high reaction rate and OFX can be degraded completely in 120min, indicated that various ions in the aquatic environment have little effect on the catalysis activity, and the catalyst can be used to deal with the water pollution universal.

Radical trapping experiments were conducted to detect the reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the OFX Fenton like degradation process over CQDs/Cu-MIO. As illustrated in Fig. 10 A, methanol, N2 and NaN3 were used as scavengers for
OH, O2
− and 1O2 to investigate their separate contribution to OFX degradation at pH=6.4 [42]. Obviously, NaN3 for 1O2 had a negligible effect on the OFX degradation. The contribution of O2
− was also not significant and its negative effect only appeared at reaction 10–60min. However, the OFX removal was greatly inhibited by 48% after reaction for 60min in the presence of methanol, which meant that
OH was the main oxidizing species in the system and has a dominate influence for the degradation of OFX. ESR spin-trap technique was further employed to confirm the ROS generated from the H2O2 decomposition catalyzed by CQDs/Cu-MIO (Fig. 10B). Obviously, the ESR spectra exhibited a 4-fold characteristic peak of DMPO-
OH adducts with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 (curve a), indicating
OH radicals were generated in CQDs/Cu-MIO-H2O2 system [43]. In comparison, there was no significant sextet peaks of DMPO-HO2
/O2
− adducts were observed. The results agreed well with the assumption of radical trapping experiments and
OH was the dominant oxidizing species responsible to the efficient degradation of OFX. Hence, the catalytic reaction mechanism needs to be clarified. Based on the XPS analysis shown in Fig. 11 , the bands centered at 709.93eV and 723.52eV assigned to Fe2+ were observed and the binding energies at 719.9eV and 724.31eV for Fe3+ appeared with a shake up satellite Fe 2p3/2 at 717.84eV [44]. Moreover, the peak at binding energy of 935.05eV could be ascribed to Cu2+ species, and Cu+ species were also identified due to the peaks at 933.85eV [45]. It indicated that the multivalent state of both Cu+/Cu2+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ existed within CQDs/Cu-MIO. The broad peak between 526eV and 534eV of O 1s was further deconvoluted into four peaks. A Fe-O signal appeared at 529.85eV and the signal at 529.04eV represented to the CuO, CO at 530.78eV and OH at 531.76eV were oxygens in functional groups of oxidized CQDs [44–46]. The main peaks at 283.98eV and 284.82eV were attributed to the CC bond with sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons orbital, the deconvoluted XPS peak of the C 1s were centered at the binding energies 285.78eV, 286.77eV, and 288.50eV were assigned to the COC, CO, and COH, respectively [46]. Hence, CQDs was successfully anchored on the surface of Cu-MIO. Hence, the excellent performance of CQDs/Cu-MIO as a heterogeneous Fenton-like catalyst may be attributed to the following three aspects: (a) high reactivity of Cu+ and Fe2+ with H2O2; (b) the spontaneous electron flow from Cu+ to Fe3+ favors the Fe3+ conversion to Fe2+ due to the multivalence of Fe and Cu; (c) the promotion on the valence change of Fe/Cu within the catalyst and the electron transfer between H2O2 and catalyst by CQDs. Based on all the experimental results, the possible heterogeneous Fenton-like reaction mechanism over CQDs/Cu-MIO was proposed as followed (Fig. 12 ).
(1)
Fe
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
Fe
3 +
+
OH +
OH
-
(2)
Fe
3 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
Fe
2 +
+
O
2
H +
H
+
(3)
Cu
+
+
H
2
O
2
→
Cu
2 +
+
OH +
OH
-
(4)
Cu
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
Cu
+
+
HO
2
+
H
+
(5)
Fe
3 +
+
Cu
+
→
Fe
2 +
+
Cu
2 +

The mesoporous structure and large surface area of CQDs/Cu-MIO provide abundant react sites, which then efficiently catalyze H2O2 into
OH and
HO2 via Eqs. 1–4. The original and generated Cu+ (Eq. (4)) can quickly reduce Fe3+ and enhance the redox cycle of Fe3+/Fe2+ as shown in Eq. (5) [47]. The electron transfer process on the surface of CQDs/Cu-MIO could efficiently catalyze H2O2 and generate enough ROS (
OH and O2
−) towards the OFX degradation. Hence, the excellent performance and stability of CQDs/Cu-MIO over a wide pH range provided a potential alternative for the remediation of OFX contamination in practical application.

The as-prepared CQDs/Cu-MIO had a typical mesoporous structure with a high dispersion of CQDs on the surface, in which 15% Cu substitution and 1.5mg/g CQDs loading rate were the optimum preparation conditions. It exhibited a high efficiency and excellent stability towards OFX degradation over a wide pH range (pH=3.6–10.0) without any extra energy input. OFX was completely degraded at only 40min,
OH was confirmed as the dominant ROS and involved in OFX degradation process. The spontaneous electron transfer between Cu+/Cu2+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ and the excellent electron transformation/energy exchange property of CQDs were responsible to the excellent performance in efficient OFX degradation of CQDs/Cu-MIO over a wide pH range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41521001) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51371162) and the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.07.049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical treatment technologies for waste-water recycling-an overview

Sonophotocatalytic treatment of ofloxacin in secondary treated effluent and elucidation of its transformation products
Chronic ecotoxic effects to Pseudomonas putida and Vibrio fischeri, and cytostatic and genotoxic effects to the hepatoma cell line (HepG2) of ofloxacin photo(cata)lytically treated solutions
Influence of the natural colloids on the multi-phase distributions of antibiotics in the surface water from the largest lake in North China
Determination of antibiotics in sewage from hospitals, nursery and slaughter house, wastewater treatment plant and source water in Chongqing region of Three Gorge Reservoir in China
Simultaneous degradation of ofloxacin and recovery of Cu(II) by photoelectrocatalysis with highly ordered TiO2 nanotubes
Enhancement of g-C3N4 nanosheets photocatalysis by synergistic interaction of ZnS microsphere and RGO inducing multistep charge transfer
State-of-the-Art progress in diverse heterostructured photocatalysts toward promoting photocatalytic performance
Sodium dodecyl sulfate modified FeCo2O4 with enhanced Fenton-like activity at neutral pH
Enhancement of Fenton degradation by catechol in a wide initial pH range
The design and development of highly reactive titanium oxide photocatalysts operating under visible light irradiation
A novel aqueous process for preparation of crystal form-controlled and highly crystalline BiVO4 powder from layered vanadates at room temperature and its photocatalytic and photophysical properties
